summary: Zhuge Liang; the Fifth Northern Expedition. Impeaches and demotes Li Yan for his falsified supply reports. Marches north again. The chapter's centerpiece: ambushes and kills Zhang He at Mumen Gorge with concealed crossbowmen on the cliffs above the road. Zhang He - the last great Wei field general from Cao Cao's generation - caught in the open and shot full of arrows.

Zhang He dies. The last surviving champion of Cao Cao's old guard, ambushed in Mumen Gorge by Zhuge Liang's hidden crossbows; falls riddled with arrows. End of an era; Sima Yi mourns. Zhang He had served from Yuan Shao's days through every major Wei campaign.
Sima Yi; against his judgment is goaded by Zhang He into pursuing - and loses Zhang He in the gorge. Continues the defensive game; refuses to engage Zhuge Liang in open battle.

Cao Zhen dies. Defeated again by Zhuge Liang's manoeuvres; falls ill and dies of mortification, the second consecutive chapter to claim a senior Wei general. Sima Yi alone holds the western front for Wei now.
Li Yan; impeached at Zhuge Liang's memorial; demoted to commoner status. The civil counterweight to Zhuge Liang in Liu Shan's court collapses; Zhuge Liang is now effectively the sole regent.
Jiang Wan; the rising bureaucrat at Liu Shan's court; will be Zhuge Liang's successor.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Gou An; named in passing - the traitor exposed last chapter.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Sun Li; on the Wei front.
Guan Xing dies. The last of the second-generation Shu young generals (after Zhang Bao) dies of illness in this chapter. End of the line for the Guan-Zhang sons. Zhuge Liang weeps when the news comes.
Qiao Zhou; in Liu Shan's court; raises portents urging caution against the campaign - ignored.
Fei Yi; in Liu Shan's court.
Yang Yi; on Zhuge Liang's staff.
Jiang Ji; on the Wei council.
Sun Chen; minor figure named in passing.

Zhuge Liang; the Sixth Northern Expedition. Inventories the famous wooden-ox-and-flowing-horse logistic devices - articulated wooden pack-animals that move along mountain roads, with internal locking pins that immobilise them on tilt; Shu uses them to outflank Wei attempts to seize the supply trains. Reaches Qishan. The chapter ends with Sima Yi cementing his refusal-to-engage; Wei dug in, Shu encamped opposite, the stage set for Wuzhang Plains.
Sima Yi; the great refusal. Cao Rui personally sends Xin Pi with the imperial flag-of-restraint to ensure Sima Yi doesn't engage, sealing his strategy. The plan: outlast Zhuge Liang. It works.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court; sanctions Sima Yi's defensive strategy.
Jiang Wei; with the expedition; rising in command authority.
Yang Yi; on Zhuge Liang's staff.
Gao Xiang; with the expedition; runs the wooden-ox supply trains.
Guan Xing; named in passing - already dead.
Qiao Zhou; in Liu Shan's court.
Fei Yi; in Liu Shan's court.
Fan Jian; in Liu Shan's court.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Sima Shi; with Sima Yi on the front; rising. The elder son who will eventually inherit command.
Sima Zhao; with Sima Yi on the front; rising.
Xiahou Ba; first major appearance - with the Wei command; the Xiahou clan's last great representative.
Xiahou He; with the Wei command.
Xiahou Wei; with the Wei command.
Xiahou Hui; with the Wei command.
Zhang Hu; with the Wei force.
Sun Li; on the Wei front.
Sun Quan; mobilises for the eastern campaign in concert with Shu.
Lu Xun; on the Wu side, prepares to attack Wei in concert with Shu.
Zhuge Jin; on the Wu side.
Sun Shao; on the Wu side.

Zhuge Liang; the chapter's two great Zhuge-Liang showpieces. (1) The Shangfang Valley fire-trap - lures Sima Yi and his two sons into a narrow valley loaded with mines and brushwood; lights the fuses; Sima Yi and his sons embrace and weep, expecting death - when a sudden thunderstorm douses the fire and saves them. Zhuge Liang weeps: 'Man proposes, Heaven disposes.' (2) The 'woman's clothes' provocation - sends Sima Yi a parcel of women's robes and a letter calling him 'an old woman who hides behind walls.' Sima Yi pretends to laugh and accepts; the Wei army's morale is shaken but Sima Yi doesn't move.

Sima Yi; narrowly escapes the Shangfang Valley fire when the storm breaks. Receives the women's clothes from Zhuge Liang and refuses to be goaded; the iron-nerved defensive game continues. Quietly examines the envoy and learns Zhuge Liang is barely eating, working day and night - concludes the campaign will end itself by Zhuge Liang's collapse.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Sun Quan; the joint Wu campaign falters at Hefei - Wu's eastern army withdraws.
Lu Xun; commands the Wu force; manoeuvres but cannot break through.
Man Chong; defends Hefei against Wu.
Wei Yan; the bait that lures Sima Yi into Shangfang Valley.
Sima Shi; with his father; nearly killed in the Shangfang trap.
Sima Zhao; with his father; nearly killed in the Shangfang trap.
Xin Pi; carries the imperial restraint flag to Sima Yi.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Xiahou Ba; with the Wei command.
Xiahou He; with the Wei command.
Xiahou Hui; with the Wei command.
Zhang Hu; with the Wei force.
Sun Li; on the Wei front.
Sun Zi; on the Wei council.
Fei Yi; in Liu Shan's court.
Liu Bei; named in passing in Zhuge Liang's reflections.

Zhuge Liang dies. The chapter's heart. At Wuzhang Plains, exhausted from years of overwork (eating only a handful of rice a day, attending to every detail of administration personally), falls ill. Attempts the seven-day prolongation ritual with seven candles arranged in the Northern Dipper pattern - Wei Yan rushes in to report Wei movements and accidentally extinguishes the main lamp; Zhuge Liang accepts the omen. Sends final orders: Yang Yi to lead the army home; Ma Dai to kill Wei Yan if he rebels; Jiang Wei to inherit the strategic legacy. Writes his last memorial to Liu Shan with simple instructions for governance. Dies. The 'fallen star' moment - a great red star streaks across the heavens as he passes; the chapter closes with the iconic image of his army withdrawing under his command even after death (the wooden effigy of him sitting in the carriage that fools Sima Yi into believing he is still alive - the famous 'dead Zhuge Liang frightens off live Zhongda').

Sima Yi; sees the great red star fall and rejoices, then advances; sees the Shu army withdraw in order with what appears to be a living Zhuge Liang at the front; panics and retreats over a dozen miles before learning he was tricked. The novel's last laugh against him.
Yang Yi; takes command of the retreat per Zhuge Liang's death-orders; brings the army home in good order. The administrative successor.
Ma Dai; given the secret sealed orders to kill Wei Yan if he rebels at the retreat. The chapter's slow-moving time bomb.

Wei Yan; the chapter's tragic figure. Bursts into the prayer tent and snuffs out Zhuge Liang's life-lamp; recognised by Zhuge Liang as the man who will rebel. Through the rest of the chapter Wei Yan chafes at Yang Yi's command of the retreat and begins making his moves for the rebellion that breaks in chapter 105.
Jiang Wei; receives Zhuge Liang's final transmission of the strategic notebooks; the chosen heir of the Hanzhong-restoration project. Will carry the burden through the next several chapters.
Jiang Wan; named at Zhuge Liang's deathbed as the next chief minister.
Fei Yi; named at Zhuge Liang's deathbed as the eventual successor to Jiang Wan.
Liao Hua; with the army on the retreat.
Wang Ping; with the army on the retreat.
Li Fu; sent by Liu Shan to Wuzhang Plains; receives Zhuge Liang's final instructions for the regency.
Li Yan; named in passing - the demoted ex-co-regent; weeps inconsolably when he hears of Zhuge Liang's death; dies of grief shortly after.
Sun Zi; in the Wei command.
Xiahou Ba; on the Wei front.
Xiahou Hui; on the Wei front.
Zhang Yi; with the army; assists Yang Yi against Wei Yan.

Wei Yan dies. Rebels against Yang Yi's command of the retreat at Hanzhong; declares his own loyalty intact but refuses to obey 'the scribe'; gathers his men and prepares to march on Chengdu. Ma Dai is openly with him - but it's a trap: per Zhuge Liang's death-order, Ma Dai turns at the prearranged signal and cuts Wei Yan down from behind in the middle of his own war cry ('Who dares kill me?'). The Wei Yan rebellion that Zhuge Liang foresaw, dispatched in a single stroke.
Ma Dai; the executioner. Has played close to Wei Yan to gain his confidence; turns and beheads him at the cue. The slow-moving time bomb finally goes off.
Yang Yi; brings the army home; finishes the Wei Yan situation; later (off-stage) is sidelined by Jiang Wan and dies in disgrace.
Jiang Wan; named senior minister of Shu in the post-Zhuge Liang reorganisation; takes over the regency.
Fei Yi; senior minister of Shu in the new arrangement.
Jiang Wei; the inheritor of Zhuge Liang's military project.
Sima Yi; in the Wei command; takes the field briefly during the Liaodong rebellion of Gongsun Yuan; subdues it; consolidates his hold on the army.

Gongsun Yuan; the Liaodong governor; declares himself King of Yan in rebellion against Wei; defeated by Sima Yi who breaks him at Xiangping. The rebellion that lets Sima Yi cement his military authority.
Guanqiu Jian; with Sima Yi against Gongsun Yuan.

Cao Rui; in the Wei court; dies at the end of the chapter. Aged 36. Names Cao Fang as heir with Cao Shuang and Sima Yi as co-regents - the arrangement that produces the High Plains Tomb coup six years later.
Empress Mao; Cao Rui's empress; deposed and forced to suicide by Cao Rui in his late illness for offending him at a banquet. The grim domestic cruelty of the end of his reign.
Sun Quan; in the Wu court.
Yang Fu; in the Wei court.
Deng Zhi; in Liu Shan's court.
Dong Yun; in Liu Shan's court.
Dong Xun; in Liu Shan's court.
Qiao Zhou; in Liu Shan's court.
Ma Jun; Wei engineer; named in passing as the inventor of the wooden ox and flowing horse - the chapter explains the mechanism. The Wei engineering counterpart to Zhuge Liang's logistics.
Li Fu; in Liu Shan's court.
Wang Ping; with the army returning from Wuzhang.
Wu Yi; in Liu Shan's court.
Zhang Ni; in the Shu ranks.
Zong Yu; in Liu Shan's court.
Zhuge Liang; named throughout in the post-mortem assessments; his tomb at Dingjun Mountain is established.
Zhuge Zhan; Zhuge Liang's son; first appearance in passing - will be the last great Shu defender at Mianzhu in chapter 117.
